timesoft
  • »
  • Level 0: Raw Data
  • View page source

Level 0: Raw Data¶

Raw data collected using the TIME Observing GUI are saved saved in netCDF files (.nc). Each time an observation is started, a new directory is created on the TIME Master computer in /data/netcdffiles/. The directory is named based on the unix time at the start of a the observation. The directory will contain at a log.txt file which is a log of the parameters that were given to the GUI when initializing the observation, and one or more .nc files containing the data for the observations.

Note

Our .nc files have a size limit, and so one observation may be split across multiple files. The loading utilities in the netCDF4 package and the timesoft.get_data will combine these files into a single data structure automatically.

We refer to these raw data files as level 0 of our data. They contain information from the telescope, the K-mirror, and the detectors, as well as some metadata. However, the sampling rates for the telescope and detector data are different. These data require some processing in order to generate timestreams with consistent timing. These tasks are handled by raw.get_data, and also wrapped in the initialization of timestreams.Timestream class instances

See the raw documentation for details on loading the level 0 data and structure of the netCDF files.

Contents of the netCDF Files¶

When a netCDF file has been loaded as a dataset using netCDF4.MFDataset, the variables in netCDF files can be accessed using keys as data_file.variable['var_name'] where var_name is replaced by the name of the variable to be accessed.

The netCDF files generated by the TIME data acquisition software contain variables related to a number of different processes, including data acquisition input parameters, telescope telemetry, housekeeping, detector readout, and K-mirror status.

Note

For most variables describing timestreams (detector readings, telemetry, etc.), the files are created by stacking groups of 100 (for detectors) or 20 (for telescope) readouts. So data arrays will have two indices corresponding to time - the first will be of variable length, indexing a group and the second will be of fixed length, indexing the position in the list of redouts.

Note

The structure of the netCDF data files is being updated prior to the Fall 2022 observing run. Some of these variables may be altered in future data.

Header Variables¶

The following collection of variables constitute header information for the observation. Most of them are generated from the inputs given to the GUI when initializing the observation.

Note

Major updates to these variables are planned prior to the Fall 2022 observing run.

Note

Due to a bug in the GUI software, many of these header values are incorrect for data from the Spring 2022 engineering run. Most information can be recovered from the log.txt files associated with each observation.

observerstr

Initials of the observer, as specified in the GUI

datamodestr

The mode for data acquisition, as specified in the GUI

rcint

The number of readout cards used, as specified in the GUI

datetimestr

Date-time string indicating the start of the observation

detectorint

The number of working detectors during the observation

framesint

The number of data acquisition frames included in the file

Housekeeping Variables¶

The following are output by the housekeeping computer and relate to sensor readouts and the timing of MCE readouts.

hk_timendarray

An array of shape (var, 2, 100) containing timing information. hk_time[:,0,:] is the unix time and hk_time[:,1,:] is the corresponding sync box number. The unix time is sparsely sampled with only ~1 value in 100 filled and the remaining entries as np.nan. Linear interpolation between the points with values can allow the matching of the other synch numbers to actual times.

hk_datandarray

An array of shape (var, 100, 252) containing sensor readouts. Most values are np.nan except when readout from a given sensor is provided at a given timestep, in which case the readout value is given.

Note

Which of the 252 entries for a given time correspond to which sensor is documented, but we do not currently know where - this will need to be tracked down at some point. We believe Jon Hunacek has this information.

Detector Variables¶

Various information related to the detectors’ status and readouts is stored in the following variables. Each MCE (MCE 0 and MCE 1) has its own set of information.

Note

Through the Spring 2022 engineering run, only MCE 0 was in use and so the MCE 1 arrays will not contain useful data

mce0_raw_data_all, mce1_raw_data_allndarray

Arrays of shape (var, 33, 32, 100) containing the raw data from each detector. The second and third indices correspond to individual detectors organized using the row-column layout. So the data for the MCE 0 detector in row 1, column 5 can be accessed mce0_raw_data_all[:,1,5,:].

mce0_header, mce1_headerndarray

Arrays of shape (var, 1700, 1). There are in fact 17 different variables stored in the header for each MCE, the first index corresponds to chunks of 100 samples, the second index is a set of 17 header variables x 100 samples per chunk. To more conveniently access the data by indexing the variable you want, you can reshape the array: mce0_header = mce0_header.reshape(-1,17,100). The variables in the header, by index, are:

  1. _footer_size

  2. _header_size

  3. _rc_present

  4. address0_ctr

  5. aux_channel

  6. data_rate

  7. frame_counter

  8. header_version

  9. num_rows

  10. num_rows_reported

  11. ramp_addr

  12. ramp_value

  13. row_len

  14. runfile_id

  15. status

  16. sync_box_num

  17. userfield

Most of these are not used in the data analysis code we have written so far, however the sync_box_num is the sync box number, which is used to determine the timestamp associated with each piece of data.

Note

Many of these variables are constant for a whole observation, we are in the process of restructuring this variable to report them only once and save space.

mce0_on_off, mce1_on_offndarray

Arrays of shape (var, 33, 32) containing ones or zeros. The value of mce0_on_off[rdout,r,c] indicates whether the detector in row r and column c was on or off during the time of readout number rdout.

Note

The on/off values of a given detector reported here will not change in the course of an observation, so mce0_on_off[0,:,:] should be the only part of the array you need. We are in the process of revising this variable to save space.

Telescope Telemetry Variables¶

The telescope sends packets of telemetry information back to the data acquisition GUI. These data are sampled at a different rate from the detector and housekeeping data, and so need to be interpolated to match the timing of other data.

telndarray

Array of shape (var,20,21). The final index access one of 21 pieces variables sent by the telescope.

  1. blanking - 0 if the telescope is turning around, 1 if it scanning across the defined map.

  2. direction - 0 if telescope is not on source, 1 if telescope is tracking field center, 2 if telescope is tracking in the -RA direction, 3 if telescope is tracking in the +RA direction 4 if telescope is turning around

  3. observing - True if data is being taken (as this is handled by the TIME data acquisiton software, not the telescope, this variable may be meaningless).

  4. empty

  5. ut - UTC time stamp

  6. lst - local siderial time (the RA presently transiting) timestamp

  7. deltaT - time since last telescope packet was sent

  8. mapra - the location of the map center (R.A.). Be aware that in the documentation provided by the ARO staff this is called cur_ra, however we have found that cur_ra and mapra are swapped in index relative to the information sheet we were given.

  9. mapdec - the location of the map center (declination). Be aware that in the documentation provided by the ARO staff this is called cur_dec, however we have found that cur_dec and mapdec are swapped in index relative to the information sheet we were given.

  10. cur_ra - the current location of the telescope (R.A.)

  11. cur_dec - the current location of the telescope (declination)

  12. raoff - the offset between current and reference positions. (mapra - raoff = cur_ra)

  13. decoff - the offset between current and reference positions. (mapdec - decoff = cur_dec)

  14. az - the current telescope azimuth

  15. el - the current telescope elevation

  16. azvelcmd - the commanded azimuth slew rate

  17. elvelcmd - the commanded velocity slew rate

  18. azvelact - the actual azimuth slew rate (this may be mislabeled)

  19. elvelact - the actual elevation slew rate (this may be mislabeled)

  20. para - the paralactic angle of the telescope

  21. unix - the unix timestamp of the data

K-Mirror Telemetry Variables¶

Information about the K-mirror system is also saved in the netCDF files. This data is kept in:

kmsndarray

A (var, 20, 4) array of K-mirror readouts. This is not fully implemented yet, but will eventually be an array of flags about the K-mirror status.

Note

Major updates to these variables are planned for some point in the future.
